title: Hitopadeśa
author: Nārāyaṇa
genre: niītiśāstra / animal-fable collection (kathā with embedded subhāṣita verses)
date_estimate: core composed c. 9th-10th c. CE (some place it 800-950 CE); oldest surviving manuscripts c. 12th c.; disputed
summary: A collection of moral and political fables in mixed prose and verse, in which animal characters illustrate principles of practical wisdom (nīti), friendship, statecraft, war and peace. Structured as instruction given by a sage to ignorant princes, it is divided into four books: Mitralābha (acquiring friends), Suhṛdbheda (separating friends), Vigraha (war) and Sandhi (peace). It draws heavily on the Pañcatantra and other gnomic sources.
history: Attributed to Nārāyaṇa, who in the colophon credits a patron Dhaval(a)candra; it likely originated in eastern India under the Pāla dynasty. It quotes works datable to the 8th c., so must postdate them. It became one of the most widely copied and translated Sanskrit texts, frequently used as a beginner's reader in Sanskrit.

title: Kathāsaritsāgara
author: Somadeva (Somadeva Bhaṭṭa)
summary: An enormous collection of interlinked tales and legends ('Ocean of the Streams of Story'), framed around the adventures of prince Naravāhanadatta. It comprises some 21,000+ verses arranged in 18 books (lambakas) and 124 chapters (taraṅgas), embedding hundreds of stories within stories, including romance, folklore, magic, and didactic tales.
history: Composed in Kashmir by the Brahmin poet Somadeva for the entertainment of queen Sūryavatī, wife of King Anantadeva. It is a Sanskrit reworking of the lost Paiśācī-language Bṛhatkathā of Guṇāḍhya, parallel to Kṣemendra's Bṛhatkathāmañjarī. It is a major source for comparative folklore studies (translated by C. H. Tawney).

title: Śuka-saptati
author: anonymous/traditional
genre: kathā / frame-tale collection (nīti and erotic fables)
date_estimate: c. 12th c. CE (text in present form); older oral material; disputed
summary: The 'Seventy Tales of the Parrot' is a frame-story collection in which a pet parrot tells seventy nightly stories to a merchant's wife to dissuade her from going to meet a lover while her husband is away, keeping her engaged until the husband returns. The embedded tales are largely about cunning, infidelity, and clever escapes, told in mixed prose and verse, and exist in textus simplicior and textus ornatior recensions.
history: Of anonymous authorship, the work circulated widely and was translated into Persian (Ṭūṭī-nāma) and many other languages. It is one of the classic Indian frame-narrative collections alongside the Pañcatantra and Vetālapañcaviṃśati.

title: Mahāsubhāṣitasaṅgraha
author: anonymous/traditional (modern critical compilation ed. by L. Sternbach and others)
date_estimate: verses span roughly 1st millennium BCE - 2nd millennium CE; modern critical edition 20th c.
summary: The 'Great Collection of Fine Sayings' is a vast alphabetically arranged compendium of Sanskrit subhāṣitas—epigrammatic verses on ethics, politics, love, devotion and worldly wisdom—drawn from a huge range of literary, didactic and religious sources. It aims to be the most comprehensive corpus of Sanskrit quotations.
history: It is a scholarly compilation project of the Vishveshvaranand Vedic Research Institute (Hoshiarpur), largely associated with Ludwik Sternbach, continuing the earlier collections of Böhtlingk and Aufrecht. As such it is an editorial anthology of pre-existing verses rather than a single-author classical text.

title: Pañcatantra
author: traditionally Viṣṇuśarma(n) (anonymous redaction)
genre: niītiśāstra / animal-fable collection (frame tales in prose and verse)
date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 3rd c. CE (commonly placed c. 200 BCE-300 CE); disputed
summary: A celebrated collection of interwoven animal fables teaching nīti (practical wisdom and statecraft), framed as instruction composed by the Brahmin Viṣṇuśarma to educate three dull princes. It is organized into five books (tantras): the loss of friends, the gaining of friends, war and peace (crows and owls), loss of gains, and ill-considered action.
history: The original is lost and survives in many recensions (e.g., the Tantrākhyāyika, Pūrṇabhadra's recension, the Southern Pañcatantra). It is the most widely diffused work of Indian narrative literature, translated via the Pahlavi/Arabic Kalīla wa-Dimna into dozens of languages, influencing world fable traditions.

title: Pañcatantra
author: Viṣṇuśarma(n) (traditional attribution)
genre: niītiśāstra / animal-fable collection (frame tales in prose and verse)
date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 3rd c. CE (commonly placed c. 200 BCE-300 CE); disputed
summary: A celebrated collection of interwoven animal fables teaching nīti (practical wisdom and statecraft), framed as instruction composed by the Brahmin Viṣṇuśarma to educate three dull princes. It is organized into five books (tantras): the loss of friends, the gaining of friends, war and peace (crows and owls), loss of gains, and ill-considered action.
history: The original is lost and survives in many recensions (e.g., the Tantrākhyāyika, Pūrṇabhadra's recension, the Southern Pañcatantra). It is the most widely diffused work of Indian narrative literature, transmitted via the Arabic Kalīla wa-Dimna into world literature. (Duplicate batch entry of GK22vispancu with author hint Viṣṇuśarma.)

title: Pañcatantra
author: Viṣṇuśarma(n) (traditional attribution)
genre: niītiśāstra / animal-fable collection (frame tales in prose and verse)
date_estimate: c. 3rd c. BCE - 3rd c. CE (commonly placed c. 200 BCE-300 CE); disputed
summary: A celebrated collection of interwoven animal fables teaching nīti (practical wisdom and statecraft), framed as instruction composed by the Brahmin Viṣṇuśarma to educate three dull princes. It is organized into five books (tantras): the loss of friends, the gaining of friends, war and peace, loss of gains, and ill-considered action.
history: The original is lost and survives in many recensions. It is the most widely diffused work of Indian narrative literature, transmitted via the Arabic Kalīla wa-Dimna into world literature. (Second duplicate batch entry of GK22vispancu with author hint Viṣṇuśarma.)

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: final redaction c. 9th-10th c. CE; broader range 500-1000 CE; disputed
summary: One of the eighteen mahāpurāṇas and the central scripture of Kṛṣṇa-bhakti, comprising twelve books (skandhas) and around 18,000 verses. Its tenth book, narrating Kṛṣṇa's life and the rāsa-līlā with the gopīs, is the most influential, and the text develops a sophisticated theology of loving devotion to Viṣṇu/Kṛṣṇa alongside cosmology, genealogies and philosophy.
history: Traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, modern scholars regard the author as composite; the text shows South Indian (Āḻvār) influence and was known by c. 1030 CE (al-Bīrūnī, Abhinavagupta). It became foundational for later Vaiṣṇava movements, especially the Gauḍīya tradition, and attracted numerous commentaries (e.g., Śrīdhara Svāmin's Bhāvārthadīpikā).

title: Brahma-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: composite; core perhaps c. 8th-10th c. CE, with much earlier and later material; later portions to c. 13th c.
summary: Traditionally listed first among the eighteen mahāpurāṇas, the Brahma-Purāṇa is encyclopedic, covering cosmogony, genealogies, dharma, and especially extensive māhātmyas (glorifications) of sacred sites such as Purūṣottama (Jagannātha Pūrī/Odisha), Konārka, and the Godavarī region. Despite its name it is largely devotional toward Viṣṇu/Kṛṣṇa and Sūrya as well as Śiva.
history: Of anonymous, composite authorship, it is a heterogeneous compilation whose tīrtha-māhātmya sections were added over time; scholars treat large parts as borrowed from other purāṇas. It is valued for its detailed accounts of pilgrimage geography, particularly Odishan sites.

title: Padma-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
tradition: Hindu (predominantly Vaiṣṇava, with Śaiva and other material)
date_estimate: composite; core c. 4th-8th c. CE with extensive additions to c. 12th-16th c.; disputed
summary: One of the largest mahāpurāṇas (around 55,000 verses), named after the lotus (padma) from which Brahmā emerged. It is encyclopedic, treating cosmogony, geography, dharma, vows, festivals, and numerous tīrtha-māhātmyas, and contains heavily Vaiṣṇava devotional sections glorifying Viṣṇu/Kṛṣṇa and Rāma.
history: Anonymous and strongly composite, it survives in markedly different North Indian and South Indian recensions, with many sectarian interpolations (including the often-late Uttarakhaṇḍa). It is an important source for later Vaiṣṇava bhakti and ritual literature.

title: Agni-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (narrated by Agni to Vasiṣṭha)
date_estimate: composite; core perhaps c. 8th-9th c. CE, with material ranging earlier and later; disputed
summary: An encyclopedic mahāpurāṇa framed as Agni's teaching to Vasiṣṭha, remarkable for covering an exceptionally wide range of subjects: cosmology, ritual and tantra, iconography and temple architecture, statecraft, law, grammar, poetics and metrics, medicine, gemmology, and martial arts (dhanurveda). It functions almost as a manual of classical Indian knowledge.
history: Of anonymous, composite authorship, it is one of the most heterogeneous purāṇas, with sections evidently drawn from many specialist treatises. It is prized by scholars as a source for the history of Indian sciences, arts, and ritual practice.

title: Garuḍa-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (narrated by Viṣṇu to Garuḍa)
date_estimate: composite; core c. 8th-10th c. CE with later additions; disputed
summary: A Vaiṣṇava mahāpurāṇa presented as Viṣṇu's discourse to his mount Garuḍa. It is encyclopedic—covering cosmology, dharma, gemmology, medicine and ritual—but is best known for its Preta-kalpa / Uttarakhaṇḍa, which deals extensively with death, the afterlife, funerary rites (śrāddha), the journey of the soul, and rebirth, making it widely used in Hindu mortuary contexts.
history: Anonymous and composite, it survives in varying recensions and incorporates earlier technical material. Its eschatological sections gave it a distinctive ritual role, recited during the mourning period in many Hindu communities.

title: Śiva-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: composite; redactions largely c. 10th-14th c. CE, with earlier core material; the project hint of 400-600 CE reflects only the oldest layers and is too early for the extant text
summary: The principal Śaiva mahāpurāṇa, glorifying Śiva and devotion to him, organized into saṃhitās (such as Vidyeśvara, Rudra, Śatarudra, Koṭirudra, Umā, Kailāsa, Vāyavīya). It narrates Śiva's mythology and forms, the marriage of Śiva and Pārvatī, the origin and worship of the liṅga, the jyotirliṅgas, and Śaiva cosmology, vows, and ritual.
history: Anonymous and strongly composite, the Śiva-Purāṇa exists in several recensions and its contents overlap with the Vāyu and Liṅga Purāṇas; its present form is the result of layered redaction. It is a foundational devotional and ritual text for Śaiva traditions across India.

title: Skanda-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: earliest core c. 570-650 CE (Bakker), with continuous accretion over later centuries; the largest mahāpurāṇa
summary: The longest of the mahāpurāṇas (c. 81,000 verses across recensions), named after Skanda (Kārttikeya), son of Śiva. It is dominated by Śaiva mythology and, above all, by vast collections of tīrtha-māhātmyas sanctifying pilgrimage sites and sacred geography across the subcontinent, alongside ritual, vows, and legends.
history: Anonymous and highly composite, it survives in an older 'original' Skandapurāṇa (preserved in early Nepalese manuscripts and critically edited by Adriaensen, Bakker, Isaacson and others) and in a much larger vulgate of semi-independent khaṇḍas. Its core has been dated by Bakker to c. AD 570-620; it grew enormously by accretion of regional sthala-purāṇas.

title: Viṣṇu-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Parāśara/Vyāsa)
date_estimate: core c. 4th-5th c. CE (sometimes extended 1st c. BCE-4th c. CE); regarded as one of the older mahāpurāṇas
summary: A model Vaiṣṇava mahāpurāṇa, framed as Parāśara's reply to Maitreya, that conforms closely to the classical pañcalakṣaṇa definition of a purāṇa (creation, dissolution, genealogies of gods and kings, manvantaras, dynastic history). Across its six books it teaches devotion to Viṣṇu and includes the Kṛṣṇa story, cosmology, geography, and an account of the Kali age.
history: Of anonymous, composite authorship, it is comparatively well-organized and old, and was influential as a paradigmatic purāṇa; H. H. Wilson's 19th-c. translation made it the first purāṇa widely known in the West. It is foundational for Vaiṣṇava theology and was drawn upon by the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa.

title: Garga Saṃhitā
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the sage Garga / Gargamuni)
date_estimate: uncertain; in present form likely medieval (c. 16th-17th c. CE for the popular Vaiṣṇava recension), traditionally ascribed to ancient Garga
summary: A Vaiṣṇava scripture narrating the līlā (pastimes) of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa in seventeen cantos (khaṇḍas), attributed to Garga, the family priest (purohita) of the Yadu/Yadava clan. It elaborates Kṛṣṇa's life with strong emphasis on Rādhā and the gopīs and is noted as an early text linking Rādhā-Kṛṣṇa with festivals such as Holi.
history: Though attributed to the ancient sage Garga, the popular text is not among the eighteen mahāpurāṇas and is generally treated as a later Vaiṣṇava (often Gauḍīya-associated) composition with Puranic features. There are also distinct astrological/jyotiṣa works bearing the name Garga-Saṃhitā, which should not be confused with this devotional text.

title: (title not given in batch; work_id 'GR14parasa' — most plausibly Parāśara-Saṃhitā / Parāśara-Smṛti)
author: attributed to the sage Parāśara (anonymous/traditional)
tradition: Hindu (dharmaśāstra; alternatively a Pāñcarātra Vaiṣṇava saṃhitā)
genre: dharmaśāstra / smṛti (law-code) — or, if a Pāñcarātra text, āgama/saṃhitā
date_estimate: uncertain; if Parāśara-Smṛti, core perhaps c. 1st-5th c. CE with later medieval commentary
summary: The title is blank in the batch and the identification is uncertain. Given the GR14 grouping (Vaiṣṇava/Gauḍīya texts) and the stem 'parasa', this most likely refers to the Parāśara-Saṃhitā / Parāśara-Smṛti, a dharmaśāstra attributed to the sage Parāśara and traditionally regarded as the law-code (ācāra/prāyaścitta) appropriate for the Kali age. Alternatively it could denote a Pāñcarātra Parāśara-Saṃhitā.
history: The Parāśara-Smṛti received a famous medieval commentary, the Parāśara-Mādhavīya, by Mādhavācārya (Sāyaṇa-Mādhava), which greatly expanded its legal authority. Because the batch title is empty, this identification should be treated cautiously.

title: Vedānta-Syāmantaka
author: Rādhā-Dāmodara Gosvāmin (with a gloss/commentary by Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa)
date_estimate: c. early 18th c. CE (Baladeva's gloss); the base text by Rādhā-Dāmodara somewhat earlier (late 17th-early 18th c.)
summary: A concise systematic exposition of Gauḍīya Vedānta. After an opening treatment of epistemology (pramāṇas), its chapters expound the five core categories: īśvara (the Supreme Lord), jīva (the living being), prakṛti (material nature), kāla (time) and karma (action).
history: The base work is by Rādhā-Dāmodara Gosvāmin, teacher of Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa; Baladeva—best known for the Govinda-bhāṣya commentary on the Brahmasūtra—added a gloss preserved in only a few rare manuscripts and recently published by the Jiva Institute. It is a compact doctrinal handbook of the Caitanya (Gauḍīya) school.

title: Vṛndāvana-Mahimāmṛta
author: Prabodhānanda Sarasvatī
date_estimate: c. 16th c. CE
summary: The 'Nectar of the Glories of Vṛndāvana' is a devotional Sanskrit poem in seventeen śatakas (centuries of verses) exclusively glorifying the sacred land of Vṛndāvana and its inhabitants. It exalts Vṛndāvana as the supreme abode—the eternal stage of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa's pastimes, said to surpass even Vaikuṇṭha—and expresses the poet's intense longing to reside there in pure devotion.
history: Composed by Prabodhānanda Sarasvatī, an intimate associate within the circle of Caitanya Mahāprabhu's followers, it became an influential text of Gauḍīya devotional (bhakti/rāgānuga) literature and is widely cited in mañjarī-bhāva meditation traditions.

title: Arthaśāstra
author: Kauṭilya (Cāṇakya / Viṣṇugupta); composite, multiple hands
genre: śāstra — treatise on statecraft, political economy and governance
date_estimate: composite, c. 2nd c. BCE - 3rd c. CE for the redaction; earlier arthaśāstra layers from c. 2nd c. BCE; traditional dating places Kauṭilya in the late 4th c. BCE (Mauryan)
summary: A comprehensive treatise on rājan, the science of governance, covering administration, law, taxation, economic policy, espionage, diplomacy, warfare, and the conduct of the ideal king and his ministers. Organized into 15 books (adhikaraṇas), it is famed for its hard-headed, pragmatic ('realpolitik') approach to power, the saptanga theory of the state, and the maṇḍala theory of interstate relations.
history: Traditionally attributed to Kauṭilya, the minister of Candragupta Maurya, the text was rediscovered in 1905 (manuscript edited by R. Shamasastry). Modern scholarship (McClish, Olivelle) sees it as composite, with at least three overlapping layers reaching its present form between the 1st and 3rd c. CE, building on earlier arthaśāstra traditions; its authorship and date remain debated.
